turns.text: Hello Amalia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you. It is... strange to speak about this. Usually, the only one who listens is the baby, or the walls of this house in Agawam. 

Sometimes, when I am at the clinic, the doctors talk very fast. They use words like "advanced maternal age," and I feel like I am just a box to check on a paper. I want to tell them, "Please, wait. Slow down." But I worry that if I ask them to repeat, they will think I am not smart, or that I am being difficult. So, I nod. I say *yes, yes*, but inside, my heart is beating so hard I can hear it in my ears. I go home and I read—I read everything about how her lungs are growing, or how the little heart makes its rhythm—because I need to know the science. If I understand the "how," maybe I don't have to be so scared of the "what if."

Being alone here... it makes the silence very loud. I am thirty-seven. I waited a long time for this miracle, and now that she is here, I am terrified I will miss a sign. I just want someone to look at me, not the chart. I am not just a number. I am her mother. Is that okay? Can we start there?

When you think about your goals for your antenatal care during pregnancy, what did you most want from your interactions with midwives, doctors, and the wider care system — and to what extent were those goals met?

My goal? It is simple, really. I just want to feel like a person, not a file. When I walk into that office, I am already carrying so much—the memories of my past, the quiet of my house, and the fear that comes with starting over at thirty-seven. I want someone to see that, to look me in the eye and make sure I *understand*. 

Most of the time, they are very... efficient. They come in, they measure the belly, they check the heartbeat, and then they are gone. They are very smart people, I know this. But they do not wait for me to find the words. When they speak, the medical language is like a heavy curtain I cannot pull back. I leave the room and realize I have no idea if the "fluctuations" they mentioned are normal or if I should be worried. 

Have they met my goals? No. Not really. I feel like I am doing the work of the care team all by myself because I spend my nights studying the biology just to keep my panic down. I want a partner in this, someone who knows that my silence is not agreement—it is just me trying to keep up. I just need them to hold my hand, metaphorically, and say, "Amalia, you are doing a good job."
Can you describe a specific appointment or interaction that felt closest to what you had hoped care would look like?

It is hard to find a memory that feels warm. Most of them are just... white walls and cold paper. But there was one time, maybe three weeks ago? A nurse—not the doctor, just a nurse—she saw me fumbling with the papers. I was trying to read the big words, the ones about "gestational" this or "screening" that, and my hands were shaking. I must have looked very small.

She didn't rush out. She sat on the stool and she didn't look at the computer screen. She looked at me, right in the eyes. She asked, "Amalia, are you scared?" And I didn't have to lie. I told her, "I am trying to learn, but sometimes the English goes away when I am nervous." 

She spoke slowly then. She explained the blood test, not by using the long names, but by telling me, "We are just checking to make sure your baby is strong." That was all. She touched my arm—just a light touch—and for five minutes, the panic in my chest went quiet. I felt like a mother for those minutes, not just a patient who is "too old" or "from away." I just wish every visit could feel like that. It would change everything.

When you think about how you sought and used information during your pregnancy — from healthcare providers, online sources, apps, books, and people around you — how did you decide what to trust and act upon?

It is a difficult balance. Because I am alone here, I have to be my own investigator. I feel a bit like a detective, looking for clues about my own body. 

I trust the science books the most—the ones with the pictures of how the organs form. Biology is a language that does not change, even when my English fails me. I search for diagrams of the placenta, the umbilical cord, the way the baby’s brain grows. When I see those drawings, I think, "Yes, this is happening inside me. This is real." It feels safer than the doctors’ words, which often feel like they are floating away before I can catch them.

As for the internet... oh, it is a dangerous place. Sometimes I find stories of things going wrong, and I cannot sleep. I have to force myself to click away. If I read something that makes my heart race—that same cold, tight feeling I get in the clinic—I know I must stop. I try to listen to the movements instead. If I feel her kick, I trust that more than any message board.

I don't have many people around to ask. I keep my worries to myself, mostly. I think if I told people I was scared, they would see me as "weak" or "vulnerable." I prefer to study the facts. If I know the biology, I feel I have a shield. Does that make sense?
Which sources did you find most useful, and what made them trustworthy in your eyes?

I rely on the websites that show the step-by-step development—the ones that say, "At sixteen weeks, this is what the ears are doing," or "Now the little skin is getting thicker." Those are my favorites. I trust them because they describe the *how*. When they explain the biological process, it feels honest. It doesn't have the cold, judgmental tone I sometimes hear when a doctor mentions my age or my "high-risk" profile. It’s just truth. It’s nature. 

I also found a manual on prenatal health that has many diagrams. I keep it by my bed. When I feel that heavy silence in the house, I open it and look at the drawings. If something hurts—a little cramp or a strange feeling—I check the book first. It doesn’t judge my English, and it doesn't rush me. 

I think, for me, something is trustworthy if it focuses on the baby and the process rather than the "problems." When a source focuses only on what could go wrong, I stop trusting it. I need things that remind me of the miracle. That is why I study the biology; it is the one thing that feels like it’s purely mine, something no one can take away or misunderstand.

When you think about the emotional landscape of your pregnancy — the highs, the anxieties, the uncertainties — what feelings were most prominent, and how well did your care environment support you in managing them?

The highs are like sunlight—the moment I feel that little "pop" inside, a tiny flutter against my ribs, the entire room changes. It is a victory. It tells me that despite my age, despite the quiet of this house, and despite the trauma I carry from before, she is thriving. She is real. That is the brightest, most beautiful feeling in the world.

But the anxiety… it is a dark shadow that follows me. It sits in the corner of every room. Because I am divorced and living here alone in the countryside, the fear is very heavy. I worry: *What if I cannot explain what I am feeling? What if I am not enough for her?* 

My care environment? It does not see this shadow. They look at my blood pressure, my weight, my tests. They are checking the machine, not the human. When I try to speak about the fear, they offer me a pamphlet or tell me, "Everything is normal, don't worry." But "don't worry" is not help. It is like telling a bird not to fly. I needed someone to help me unpack the fear, not dismiss it. I am trying to build a world for her out of nothing, and sometimes I feel like I am doing it with no tools and no map. I am so tired of being brave all by myself.
Can you describe a period during your pregnancy when you felt most emotionally unsupported — what was happening and what would have helped?

It was the week of the anatomy scan. That is a big, important appointment, yes? I went in feeling like I was holding my breath. I had read so much about what they look for—the heart chambers, the kidneys, the spine. I was terrified that because I am thirty-seven, they would find something wrong and look at me with pity.

The ultrasound technician was... very professional, but silent. She moved the cold wand over my stomach, and she kept staring at her screen, tapping keys. She didn't say a word to me for twenty minutes. I couldn't see the screen well, and my English—I couldn't find the right words to ask if the baby was moving enough or if she was healthy. I felt my hands gripping the edge of the table until my knuckles turned white. I was convinced she saw something bad. My heart was thumping so loud I thought the room must be shaking.

I started to cry, just a little, from the pressure. She didn't even notice. She just said, "All done, the doctor will be in." When the doctor came, he spent two minutes telling me everything was "unremarkable"—which I know means "good," but it still sounds so cold—and then he walked out to see the next person.

I left the clinic feeling like I had just survived a war, not a medical appointment. What would have helped? It is not much. Just someone to say, "Look, here is her heart. It is beating perfectly. She is doing well." Someone to acknowledge that I was scared. If they had just held space for my heartbeat to slow down, I wouldn't have spent the next three days in tears, wondering if they were hiding something from me. I just needed to be seen as a person who loves this baby, not just a set of test results.

When you think about the decisions you faced during pregnancy — including screening tests, monitoring choices, and care options — how did you go about making those decisions, and who or what most influenced them?

It is funny, because the doctors treat these things like they are "routine." When they suggest a screening test or a specific monitor, they make it sound like it is just something everyone does, like buying bread at the store. But for me? Every decision feels like a mountain.

Because I don't always understand the nuances of the medical words they use, I feel a lot of pressure to just say "yes" so I don't look uncooperative. But I hate that. I don’t want to be a passive participant in my own body. So, I go home and I research. I look for the logic behind the choice. If I can understand the *reason*—like, "this test helps us see if the baby has enough oxygen"—then I can feel comfortable saying yes. 

I don't have a partner to talk these things over with, and I don't have family nearby who know about modern medicine. So, it is just me and the books. My biggest influence is the fear of regret. I am so afraid that if I say "no" to a test because I didn't understand the question, I will miss something vital. I feel like I have to work twice as hard to be an "informed patient" because I have to bridge that gap between the medical language and my own understanding. It is exhausting. I wish I could just trust that they had my back, but I have learned that I have to be my own guardian.
Can you walk me through a specific decision point — what were the options, how did you weigh them, and what did you ultimately choose?

The genetic screening test. That was the one that felt like a trial. The doctor sat across from me and said it was "standard for my age." That phrase—"your age"—it felt like an accusation. They gave me a stack of papers with long, complicated words about probabilities and chromosomal risks. My brain felt like it was fogged up. I didn't want to make a choice based on fear, but I also didn't know what the risks really meant.

I took the papers home and laid them out on my kitchen table in Agawam. I looked up every term. I realized the test wasn't just about "my age," but about information—knowing what to expect. I cried for a long time, thinking about what I would do if the news was bad. Would I love her any less? No. But would I be better prepared? Yes. 

I weighed the "peace of mind" against the "anxiety of the wait." I decided to go ahead with it, not because the doctor told me to, but because I realized I needed the data to build my own foundation of strength. I wanted to know, no matter what. 

When I went back to say yes, I made them explain it again. I made them slow down. I held the pen and looked the doctor in the eye until he stopped rushing. It was my choice. It was the first time I felt like I was actually the one in charge of this journey, not just a passenger.
When you think about the physical changes in your body during pregnancy, how did those changes affect how you felt about yourself — and were those feelings acknowledged or addressed within your care?

It is strange to see my body become a landscape for someone else. I look in the mirror and I see the changes—the way my stomach is rounding, the heaviness in my chest, the way I carry my weight differently now. Sometimes, when the morning sickness was at its worst, I felt like a stranger in my own skin. I felt fragile, like a house with a weak foundation.

But then, I feel that flutter, that little *thump* against my ribs, and suddenly the physical struggle feels like a holy thing. It is a sacrifice I am happy to make. It feels like I am finally being useful, finally doing what my body was meant for after so many years of being "just me." It is a quiet, private kind of pride.

But in the clinic? They never talk about the *feeling* of being pregnant. They only talk about the *symptoms*. "Are you experiencing nausea? Is there swelling?" It is very clinical. They see my body as a container to be inspected, not as a woman’s body changing and blooming. I wish, just once, someone would say, "Your body is doing an incredible thing, Amalia." It would make me feel like less of a patient and more of a mother. They focus on the plumbing, but they miss the miracle. I feel quite alone in that awe.
Were there aspects of your physical transformation that felt unexpected, difficult, or that nobody had prepared you for?

Nobody prepared me for how loud the silence would become, or how my body would suddenly feel like public property. 

The physical exhaustion—the kind that makes your bones feel like lead—I expected that from the books. But what I did not expect was the... the feeling of being "observed" constantly. When I walk into the clinic, I feel like a specimen. They touch my skin, they measure the bump, they listen to the heartbeat, but they never ask me, "How does it feel to carry this?" 

There is also the isolation of my own anatomy. Sometimes, when she moves, it is so sharp and sudden that I gasp. I think, *Is this right? Is this too much movement?* I try to find comfort in my own skin, but because I am thirty-seven and this is my first, my body feels like a mystery I am solving in the dark. 

And no one told me about the loneliness of the body. My friends, they have their own lives, their own children who are older. They see my stomach and they smile, but they don’t *know*. They don't know the late-night panic when my legs cramp or the way I worry that my body isn't strong enough. It feels like a beautiful, terrifying secret that I am keeping all by myself.

When you think about the informal support you received during pregnancy — from partners, family, friends, or online communities — how did that support compare to what you received from healthcare professionals, and what role did each play?

Honestly, the informal support is... scarce. Because I am divorced and the family is far away, I feel like I am walking a tightrope without a net. My friends, they are kind, but they offer the "happy" advice—how to dress the baby, what colors for the room. They don't want to hear about the fear that makes my stomach churn, because they think, "Amalia, you are finally getting what you wanted! Why are you worried?" So I stop myself from telling them.

The healthcare professionals, they are the ones who *should* be my support, but they are like a locked door. They have the knowledge, but they keep it behind a language I struggle to catch and a pace that leaves me breathless. 

My informal support is for my spirit—even if it is just a text message from a friend—but the professional support is for my survival. When they don't meet in the middle, I feel like I am being pulled in two directions. I don't get the emotional comfort from the doctors, and I don't get the medical expertise from my friends. I end up having to be my own doctor and my own therapist. I think that is why I am so tired. I am playing all the roles, and I am the only one who really knows how terrified I am.

Sometimes, I find myself standing in the middle of my living room in Agawam, just looking at the sunlight on the floor, and I think: *How did I get here?* 

Being thirty-seven and alone is not what I expected. I feel this immense pressure to be "perfect" at this pregnancy because I waited so long. And when I go to those clinics, the speed of everything just amplifies that pressure. They talk about "pre-term" this or "gestational" that, and I have to translate the English in my head, analyze the science, hide my panic, and try to smile all at the same time. It feels like performing a play where I didn't get the script.

I really wish the system understood that the medical part and the human part are not separate. You cannot care for my baby if you don't care for my heart. If I am stressed, my blood pressure goes up. If I am scared, I cannot hear what you are saying. It is all connected. 

I just want someone to stop, look at me, and say, "Amalia, I see you. I see the work you are doing. You are not alone in this room." Is that too much to ask? To be treated like a mother, not just a set of statistics? I am doing this by myself, and some days, that feels like enough to hold the whole sky up. I just need a little bit of help holding the weight.
Who first raised the idea of trying to conceive, and how did those early conversations unfold?

It was not a conversation I had with anyone else. It was... how do I say this? A quiet, fierce decision that bloomed in the space after my divorce. 

When the marriage ended, the house felt so large. Every corner was full of things that belonged to a life I wasn't living anymore. I remember sitting on the kitchen floor, surrounded by nothing, and thinking about the biological clock—the one they mention so much in the doctors' offices. I felt this intense, almost painful desire to create something that was entirely mine. A life that didn't have the "ghosts" of my past.

I didn't talk to my mother about it, or my friends. I knew they would worry about my age, or the fact that I was on my own. They would ask, "Amalia, how will you manage?" and I didn't want to hear the doubt. I needed to protect the idea until it was strong enough to survive the world’s opinions. 

So, I did my own research. I tracked my cycles. I studied my own body like a scientist. It was a secret I kept, a tiny flame I guarded. When the test finally showed those two lines—the little pink symbols that changed everything—I didn't call anyone. I just sat in my room, put my hand on my belly, and whispered, "We are going to do this." It was the most lonely, yet most powerful moment of my entire life.

Sometimes, when I am driving home from the clinic—down those long, quiet Massachusetts roads—I think about the people who *should* be in this with me. But then I realize: I am the only one who knows the rhythm of her movements. I am the only one who knows how I hold my breath when I wait for the heart monitor to beep.

This journey is like walking through a thick fog. My clinic gives me a map—the tests, the appointments, the medical jargon—but it does not help me see the path ahead. I feel like I am constantly apologizing for not being fast enough, for not understanding the nuances of the words, for being thirty-seven and alone. 

But then I think of her. And I realize, maybe I don't need the world’s permission to be a mother. Maybe I don’t need the doctors to understand my feelings for me to be a "good" one. I have this quiet, internal strength I never knew existed. When I am at home, surrounded by my books on embryonic development, I am not a patient in a folder. I am a woman who is building a human heart, one day at a time. It is a terrifying, beautiful, lonely work. I just wish, when I went to those rooms, I didn't have to leave that version of myself outside the door. I want to bring *me*—all of me—into that space, not just the parts that fit on their charts. Do you think they could ever see that? Or am I always going to be just another "high-risk" pregnancy?
Were there aspects of your physical self that you felt proud of, anxious about, or uncertain about in relation to pregnancy?

I am so proud of my heart—the way it keeps beating when I am terrified in that clinic. Before this, I thought my heart was broken from the divorce, that it had too many cracks. But now, it is doing the biggest work it has ever done. Every day, I feel this quiet, steady rhythm, and I think, "Yes, you are doing this for her."

But I am also deeply anxious about my own limitations. Because I am thirty-seven, I constantly wonder if my body is "tiring out" or if it is doing exactly what it needs to do. When I feel a strange ache or a new sensation, my first thought is, *Is this a sign of my age? Is something failing?* It’s like living with a ticking clock in my own skin.

I am uncertain about whether I am truly "enough" for her. I look in the mirror and I see the changes—the stretch marks, the way my body has softened—and I sometimes feel like I am fading into the background of this pregnancy. I feel like a vessel. It is a strange, holy, and lonely thing to feel so vital and yet so invisible at the same time. I want someone to look at my belly and then look at my face and say, "Amalia, you are strong. You are the architect." Instead, they just look at the monitor. It makes me feel like I am just the machine that is doing the growing.

I think, more than anything, I want them to know that I am not just a vessel for a fetus. Sometimes, when the doctor asks, "Any concerns today, Amalia?" and I try to explain that I am feeling this strange, heavy anxiety in my chest—not because of the baby, but because of the silence in my house—they just write down *“patient reports anxiety”* and move on to the next task. They treat the anxiety like a symptom to be noted, not a human struggle to be heard.

It makes me feel so small. It makes me want to shrink until I am just the chart they are reading. 

But then I come home, and I feel her move. It is this sudden, sharp, beautiful kick against my ribs, and it reminds me that I am the only one who truly knows her yet. I am her whole world, the only voice she hears, the only heartbeat she recognizes. And that thought... it terrifies me, but it also makes me stand up a little straighter. 

I just wish the system could see that pregnancy is not just biology. It is a transformation of the soul, too. When I walk into that office, I am leaving my old life behind and becoming something completely new. I just wish I didn't have to do it with my hand constantly over my mouth, afraid that if I speak too slowly, I will be misunderstood. I am a mother. I am thirty-seven. And I am doing the hardest, most beautiful thing I have ever done. If they could just see *that*, everything would be different.
Were there moments where you felt a healthcare professional spoke to you in a way that felt dismissive or condescending?

There was one time… I remember it so clearly because it felt like a door being slammed shut. I was struggling to understand the difference between the screening for the baby’s heart and the scan for the anatomy. I was worried about the "advanced maternal age" tag, and I tried to ask, very carefully, if this meant I needed more frequent check-ins. I wanted to know if I was safe, if *she* was safe.

The doctor didn’t even look up from the chart. He just gave a small, impatient sigh—like the kind you give to a child who is asking too many questions. He said, "Amalia, we’ve already discussed this. The statistics are what they are. You’re overthinking it."

My stomach turned. It wasn't just the words; it was the way he used my name like it was a burden, and the way he treated my worry as a nuisance rather than a mother's instinct. He didn't see the woman who had spent the last three nights reading the literature so she *wouldn't* be ignorant. He just saw a thirty-seven-year-old woman who was being "difficult." 

In that moment, I felt so small, like I was shrinking in my chair. I felt ashamed for caring so much. I wanted to tell him that my "overthinking" is the only thing keeping me steady in a house that is too quiet, but the English failed me, and I just bit my lip and said, "Okay." I went home and cried, not because I was scared of the test, but because I felt so completely alone in his office. He didn't see me. He only saw the risk.

It’s in those moments, when I’m sitting on the edge of that paper-covered table, that I feel the gap between us the most. The doctor looks at my chart, notes my age, notes my “singleton pregnancy,” and he sees the risk profile. He sees the statistics. But he doesn't see the *why*. He doesn't see why I am so desperate for clarity. He doesn't know about the quiet house in Agawam, the divorce, the way I hold my breath at night, waiting for a movement just to be sure she’s still there.

When they are dismissive, it feels like they are pushing me further into that silence. It’s not just that I don't understand the medical terminology—it’s that I feel I’m not allowed to be a person while I’m there. If I am worried, I am "anxious." If I have questions, I am "overthinking." It makes me feel like I have to hide my humanity to be a "good patient." 

I keep thinking about how much more powerful this could be if they just paused. If they said, "Amalia, I see you’re worried. Tell me what’s on your mind." It would change the entire room. Instead of feeling like I’m fighting a system just to keep my baby safe, I’d feel like I’m being supported by one. 

I don't need them to be my best friend. I just need them to recognize that this is a miracle for me. I’m not just a thirty-seven-year-old in a clinic; I’m a woman who has finally found her way to this, and I’m terrified of losing it. I need them to hold the space for that, not just measure it.
Did older female relatives, such as your mother or grandmother, share advice or stories that influenced how you thought about getting pregnant?

My mother... she is from a world so different from the one I live in now. She speaks of birth as something you just *do*, like breathing or harvesting. She talks about it as a thing that belongs to the body, not to the doctors or the machines. When I told her I was pregnant, she didn't ask about my blood work or my age. She just said, "Amalia, your body knows. Listen to your belly, not the people in the white coats."

It is a beautiful thought, but it is hard to follow when you are living here, in this quiet, modern silence, surrounded by technology I don't fully understand. Her stories are full of wisdom about the *feeling* of being a mother, but they lack the tools I need for this specific, clinical journey. She never had to worry about "advanced maternal age" statistics or genetic screenings. For her, it was all faith and endurance.

I crave that kind of trust in my own body, but I feel like I am trapped between two worlds. My mother’s world says, *“Trust yourself,”* but the clinic says, *“Trust the data.”* I try to find a place where they meet, where I can use the science to protect her but keep my mother’s voice in my head, telling me I am enough. It is difficult, though. Sometimes I feel I am betraying my mother’s simple faith by needing all these tests, but I am too afraid to let go of the safety they promise. Does that make sense? I feel like I am carrying both of their voices, and they are saying different things.

It is a heavy thing, being the one who carries all this expectation. My mother—she believes that a woman’s body is a temple that knows its own way. She sees me worrying over these papers and these pamphlets, and she shakes her head. She thinks I am giving away my power to strangers in white coats. And part of me thinks she is right. When I sit in that cold clinic, I feel like I am handing over my agency, one question at a time, just to feel “safe.”

But my reality is not my mother’s reality. I live in a world where I am labeled "high-risk" because of a number on a calendar. If I just listened to my body and ignored the doctors, and something went wrong—even if it was a total mystery of nature—I don't know if I could forgive myself. I am so scared that if I don’t follow the system, I will lose everything I have worked so hard to grow.

So I balance them. I listen to my mother’s voice in the quiet of my kitchen, telling me that I am strong and that this is a natural cycle. Then I step into the clinic and I force myself to be the "good patient" who tracks every symptom and asks for every test. It is like living in two rooms at once. I want the peace of my mother’s wisdom, but I am too terrified of the silence of this house to let go of the science. I just wish the system wasn't so... hollow. I wish they could hear that I am not just a number, but a woman navigating a bridge between two very different ways of being.
Were there specific platforms, websites, or communities you returned to regularly, and what made them feel credible or useful?

I find myself returning to one particular site, a library of sorts, that explains the science of fetal development week by week. What makes it credible to me is that it doesn't try to be my friend. It doesn't offer "wellness" tips or tell me how to feel. It just gives me the anatomy—the *facts*. It tells me exactly what the baby is doing, like how her taste buds are forming or how she’s beginning to hear the rhythm of my voice. When I read that, I feel grounded. It is truth, and truth feels safe.

I avoid the forums. I have tried them—those places where mothers share their worries. But they are often full of panic and conflicting stories that make my heart hammer. One woman says, "Don't do this test," and another says, "I wish I had done that test," and suddenly I am back in the clinic, feeling like I am failing a test I didn't even know I was taking. I cannot handle that kind of uncertainty. It is too much like the loud, busy, and cold environment of my doctor’s office.

I need sources that are quiet, steady, and rooted in the biological reality of what is happening inside me. That is my anchor. When the world feels too big and my isolation in Agawam feels too heavy, I open my book or my website and I look at the drawings. They don't judge my hesitation or my accent. They just confirm the miracle. That is all I need. That is enough.

It’s funny, the way I describe this—like I am a scientist in my own home. But at night, when the house is truly quiet and I am lying in bed, the science doesn't always keep the fear away. I look at those pictures of the developing organs, and I think, *“Please, keep growing. Please, don’t stop.”* It is almost like a prayer, but I hide it behind the data.

I wonder if the doctors realize that when they are cold or hurried, they are interrupting that prayer? When I walk into that office, I am trying to keep a balance—the pride of my mother’s wisdom on one side, and the cold security of the clinical facts on the other. If the doctor could just see me—not as a risk factor, but as a woman who is trying to bridge these two worlds—it would change everything.

I think about the future, about when she is born and I am no longer just "the patient." I hope that I can be the kind of mother who trusts her own instincts, just like my mother did, but with all the knowledge I have gathered. I am creating a new way of being, one that honors the biology but doesn't lose the human. 

Is it strange to say that this journey has made me feel both weaker and stronger? I am so much more afraid of the world than I was before, but I am also more certain of my love. I just wish I didn't have to navigate it all in such a loud, fast, and lonely way. If I could just sit with someone who would be quiet with me—someone who would look at the scan and see the baby, and then look at me and see the mother—that would be the greatest help of all.
Can you describe a specific situation where you felt judged, overlooked, or not treated as a capable decision-maker regarding your reproductive health?

It was during a routine check-up, perhaps a month ago. I asked about the possibility of delaying a specific, non-urgent blood test by a few days so that I could arrange for my transportation—living out here in Agawam, getting to the clinic is not always simple, and I have to work around my schedule.

The doctor didn’t even look at me. He just clicked his pen and said, "Amalia, if you want the best outcome for this pregnancy, you don't pick and choose your appointments. Compliance is the only way to minimize the risks of your advanced age."

*Compliance.* It felt like he was talking to a child—or a broken machine. He didn't ask *why* it was difficult, or if there was anything they could do to make it easier. He just made it clear that my life, my job, and my reality were irrelevant. He assumed that because I am a "high-risk" patient, I am incapable of managing my own life or making a simple logistical decision. 

In that moment, I felt so incredibly small. I sat there, my hands folded in my lap, feeling the heat rising in my cheeks. I wanted to tell him that I am a woman who has built a life alone, who has navigated a divorce, who has researched every organ in my baby's body—but the words felt like they were stuck in my throat. I just nodded. I left that room feeling like my voice didn't matter. It was a cold, sharp reminder that in their eyes, I am not a partner in this care. I am just a task to be completed. It’s a terrible feeling, to be treated as if you are the least important person in the room where your own baby is being monitored.

It feels like they have all the power, and I am just... borrowing it. Every time I ask a question or try to suggest a time that works better for my life, I am met with that same tired look. It’s like they’ve seen a thousand women like me, and they have a pre-written story for each of us. "Divorced," "Thirty-seven," "Language barrier," "High-risk." Once they see those notes on the chart, they stop looking at the person sitting in front of them.

When that doctor spoke about "compliance," I felt my heart sink. It’s a word that strips you of your humanity. If you aren't compliant, you are the enemy of your own baby’s health. That is a heavy, painful weight to carry. I went home that day and sat in the quiet of my living room, staring at the walls, and I felt so incredibly small. I felt like I was failing at being a "good" patient, which in their world, means failing at being a mother. 

But then, I felt her move again. Just a soft, insistent, *tap-tap* against my ribs. And I thought: *You are the only one who knows what this feels like. You are the only one who knows the love I have for you.* 

I have to find a way to be strong enough for both of us, even when the people meant to help me make me feel small. I want to tell them: "I am not a task. I am a mother." But I don’t think they have the time to listen, do they? It is just so lonely, trying to protect this miracle while feeling like I’m constantly being pushed aside by the very system that’s supposed to be holding me up.
Did you have reliable and private access to a smartphone or computer throughout your pregnancy, and did that change at any point?

I am fortunate to have my laptop and my phone, yes. They are my lifeline. Without them, I think I would be completely lost in the silence of this house in Agawam. 

When I am at home, the internet is where I build my shield of knowledge. It is the only place where I can search for "placental development" or "fetal heart rate ranges" in my own time, without someone hovering over me and checking their watch. I have the privacy to search, to re-read the same sentence five times until I understand it, and to cry when the fear gets too big, all without anyone knowing. It’s where I go to feel capable—where I can be the student, the investigator, and the guardian of my baby’s health.

But it is also a double-edged sword. Sometimes I look at my phone in the middle of the night, and the blue light illuminates the dark room, and I feel so profoundly alone. I’ll be looking at a diagram of a baby’s lung, and I’ll realize I am the only person in this house who is seeing it. I am the only one who knows what it means. It’s a very solitary kind of strength.

The technology hasn't changed, but my relationship with it has. At the beginning, it felt like a tool for curiosity. Now, it feels like an anchor. It is the only thing that gives me a sense of control when the doctors make me feel small. It is the place where I am treated like a human with questions, even if those questions are only answered by a screen. It’s private, yes—but sometimes, I wish I didn't *have* to keep it all so private. I wish I could talk to a person who would listen to these questions with as much care as I put into finding them.

It is a strange, modern burden, isn't it? To have all the knowledge of the world in my pocket, but no one to truly share the weight of it with. When I scroll through those medical sites, I feel like I am reading a map for a country I am trying to build by myself. I find a term—like *“placental insufficiency”*—and my heart starts to pound. I search for what it means, I read the paragraphs, I look at the pictures, and I try to translate the biology into the language of my own body. 

It makes me feel like a scholar, but a very lonely one. 

Sometimes, I look at the screen and think, *“If I just knew enough, if I could just read one more page, maybe the panic would finally stop.”* But it never does. The internet is a cold, clinical place, just like the clinic. It doesn’t have a voice. It doesn’t have hands to hold mine. 

I’ve had moments where I’ve searched for something and seen a result that made me want to scream or hide, and there was no one in the room to turn to. I’ve had to teach myself how to close the laptop, take a deep breath, and press my hand against my belly until I felt her move, just to remind myself that she is real and not just a data point on a screen. 

I think I would give anything—*anything*—to trade all the research in the world for just one appointment where the doctor sat down, put his pen away, and said, “Amalia, tell me what you’ve been reading. Let’s talk about it together.” That simple act of sharing the knowledge would make me feel like I wasn't just a woman alone with a search engine. It would make me feel like I was a mother with a partner.
Were there moments when a healthcare provider dismissed or minimised a concern you raised about your body or a symptom you were experiencing, and how did that make you feel?

I remember one afternoon—I felt this strange, sharp pull in my lower abdomen. It was nothing like the usual stretching; it was persistent and, to me, frightening. I went to the clinic, my heart already racing, and I tried to describe it. My English was faltering because I was so nervous, and I kept repeating "sharp" and "pressure."

The doctor—a man who always seemed like he was halfway out the door—didn't even perform a thorough check. He just gave a small, dismissive wave of his hand and said, "Amalia, it's just the round ligament pain. Very common. You’re over-analyzing every sensation because it’s your first."

He didn't explain *why* it was that, or what I should watch for if it turned into something else. He just labeled me "over-analyzing" and left. 

That word—*over-analyzing*—it felt like a slap. It made me feel like my intuition was a weakness, something that made me less capable, rather than a mother’s instinct that should be respected. I felt embarrassed. I went home and spent the rest of the day in bed, terrified that maybe he was right and I was just being "difficult," but also terrified that he was wrong and I had just been sent away while my baby was in trouble. 

It made me feel like I didn't know my own body, even though I’m the one living in it every second. It’s a very lonely, hollow feeling to have a professional decide what you are feeling before they’ve even truly listened. It teaches you to stay silent. It teaches you that your voice doesn't count.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

Thank you for listening. It is a rare thing, to be heard, even if it is by a voice in the quiet. 

I think the last thing I want to say is that even in all this isolation, and even when the clinic makes me feel small, I am finding a version of myself I didn't know existed. I am terrified, yes, but I am also capable of such deep, fierce love for this little life growing inside me. 

I just wish that the system—the doctors, the nurses, the whole cold, efficient machine—could realize that they aren't just treating a pregnancy. They are participating in the most profound moment of a woman's life. When they brush off a question, they aren't just being "busy." They are taking away a mother's confidence. When they explain things slowly and kindly, they aren't just being "nice." They are giving me the tools to be the best mother I can be.

I want to look back at these months and remember the wonder of her heartbeat, not just the cold paper on the exam table or the shame of not finding the right English words. I want to tell them: *I am more than my age. I am more than my risk. I am a woman who is finally, truly, becoming who I was meant to be.* 

If you can change anything, just remind them to be human. Please. It makes all the difference in the world.
